items.correct: Differences in seawater density driven by BOTH temperature (thermo) and salinity (haline) — cold, salty water sinks at high latitudes, driving a global ocean conveyor belt over centuries.
items.misconception: Thermohaline circulation is driven only by temperature differences in the ocean.
items.random_wrong: Thermohaline circulation is driven by the wind blowing across the ocean surface.
items.why: Salinity is the other half of the name and the mechanism. Freshwater influx (from melting ice) reduces salinity and could slow the circulation — a key climate change concern (the AMOC weakening).

items.q: What is the difference between hard and soft engineering for flood management?
items.correct: Hard engineering uses built structures (dams, levees, channelisation) to control flow; soft engineering works with natural processes (afforestation, wetland restoration, floodplain zoning).
items.misconception: Hard engineering always works better than soft engineering at preventing floods.
items.random_wrong: Soft engineering means using softer materials like sand instead of concrete.
items.why: Hard engineering is expensive, ecologically damaging and can shift problems downstream. Soft approaches are cheaper and sustainable but slower to act — a typical evaluation question.

items.q: What is the sequence of headland erosion from crack to stump?
items.correct: Crack to cave to arch to stack to stump — waves widen cracks into caves, two caves meet to form an arch, the arch collapses leaving a stack, and the stack erodes to a stump.
items.misconception: Stacks form where rocks fall from the cliff into the sea.
items.random_wrong: Stacks form when the sea level rises and floods a hill.
items.why: It's a sequence of erosion at the same site, not random rockfall or sea level rise. Each landform is the predecessor of the next, with hydraulic action and abrasion driving the progression.

items.q: What process causes corals to lose their colour under heat stress?
items.why: It's Bleaching. “Acidification” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which contributes about half of recent sea-level rise?
items.why: Correct answer: Thermal expansion. “Tectonic uplift” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate current rate of sea-level rise (mm/year)?
items.why: The real figure is 3.3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
